0.body: Interp is currently in beta testing ! Don't use it for production! Interp is a procedural general-purpose dynamically typed interpreted scrpiting language. Although it uses duck-typing, it also enforces type safety. The interplang interpreter is coded entirely in Rust, and the language can be extended by dynamic foreign functions programmed in pure Rust. Functions in Interp are first-class-citizens and can be assigned to variables and passed as arguments to other functions. Interp supports both 64-bit integers as well as 64-bit floating point numbers. The keytable in Interp is a map of sorts, with keys and values, these can be of whatever type. There are no classes, It's not an object oriented language!
0.title: Interp, the Language

10.body:
mod animals { atom Dog; atom Cat; enum AnimalType { Dog, Cat } struct Animal { type AnimalType, name String, age Int[0..], } fun Animal(type AnimalType, name String, age Int[0..]) Animal { var obj = { type=type, name=name, age=age }; ret obj; } } var bob = animals::Animal(animals::Cat, "Bob", 2); Print(bob);
# output: {"type" = Atom("animals::Cat"), "name" = "Bob", "age" = 2} Read more about modules , Atoms , Enums , Structs , Integers , Strings , Keytables , Functions , variables , or Print
10.title: Animal

11.title: ForEach

12.body: Interp implements the following primitives. They can be stored into variables as well as they can be passed into functions as arguments. Void Null String Int Float Bool List Keytable Function
12.title: Values

14.title: Denoting 'nonexistent'

15.breadcrumbs: Primitives » Void » As a function return value
15.title: As a function return value

16.body: A list can't have Void as an item. When a List index returns Void it means the end of the List.
16.title: Lists

17.body: Void is the only Value that does not fit in to the Any type .
17.title: Any type

18.body: Null (keyword null) means that the variable exists, but has no value.
18.title: Null

19.title: As a Type

20.body: Unlike Void , Lists can contain Null.
20.title: In a List

21.body: A String is a sequence of characters. They are encoded in UTF-8. A String literal is defined like so: var string = "My String"; Strings can be printed with the Print function. Print("Hello, world!");
21.title: String

22.body: Strings can be indexed with Ints , index 0 is the first element. string[3] # fourth unicode character
22.title: Indexing

23.body: You can concatenate Strings with the + operator. Print("Hello, " + "world!");
23.title: Concatenation

24.body:
Strings can be formatted with the Format function. Print(Format("{} + {} = {}", 1, 2, 1 + 2));
# Output = 1 + 2 = 3
24.title: Format

25.body: Ints are 64-bit signed integers. var int = 123;
25.title: Int

26.body:
The following mathematical operators are supported 1 + 2 # Addition
1 - 2 # Subtraction
1 * 2 # Multiplication
1 / 2 # Division
1 % 2 # Remainder
1 ^ 2 # Power
26.title: Math

27.body: Value ranges can be expressed as a type. Int[0..10] accepts an Int that is >= 0 & < 10.
27.title: As a type

28.body: Floats are 64-bit floating-point nubmers. var float = 1.23;
28.title: Float

29.body:
The following mathematical operators are supported 1 + 2 # Addition
1 - 2 # Subtraction
1 * 2 # Multiplication
1 / 2 # Division
1 % 2 # Remainder
1 ^ 2 # Power
29.title: Math

30.body: Value ranges can be expressed as a type. Int[1.2 .. 3.4] accepts a Float that is >= 1.2 & < 3.4.
30.title: As a type

31.body:
Booleans are either true or false. The most common use is the If statement . if bool { Print("bool == true");
}
31.title: Bool

32.body:
Bools can be crafted with some operators. a == b # Equation
a != b # Negative Equation
a | b # Or
a & b # And
a < b # Less Than
a > b # More than
a <= b # Less Than or Equals
a >= b # More Than or Equals
a is Type # Fits into Type
a is not Type # Does not Fit into Type
32.title: Operations

33.body: Lists are sequences of values. var list = [1, "str", true, [2.3]];
33.title: List

34.body: Lists can be indexed with Ints , index 0 is the first element. list[0] Negative indicies start from the end, index -1 is the last element. list[-1]
34.title: Indexing

35.body: Lists can be concatenated with the + operator. [1, 2, 3] + [4, 5, 6] == [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
35.title: Concatenation

36.body:
List length can be checked with the Len function. Print(Len([1, 2, 3]));
# output: 3
36.title: Length

37.body:
To match all lists, simply use List . To match a list which all values match a type, you can use a generic type argument. fun TakesIntList(arg List<Int>) { Print(arg)
}
37.title: As a Type

38.title: Keyable

39.title: KeyValuePairs

4.body:
You can run Interp files (*.it) with the interp binary. Create a file called hello.it: Print("Hello, world!"); Then just simply run the command: $ interp hello.it
Hello, world!
4.breadcrumbs: Hello, world! » Execute a source-code file
4.title: Execute a source-code file

40.body: A variable has a name and holds a value. When a variable is referenced but does not exist Void is returned. Variables are defined with the var keyword. var variable = "I'm the Value!"; After defining variables can be reassigned. Variables themselves do not have types designated for them. variable = true; Variables can be passed into functions . The value is copied. Function(variable); Functions can also take the reference of a variable, this way, the value is not copied. Function(ref variable);
40.title: Variables

41.title: In-place operations

42.title: Functions

43.body:
Default values for paramters are allowed. Parameters can also be referred with their name. fun Function(int foo, string bar = "default_string", baz Bool = false) { ...
} Function(1, baz=true) # allowed
43.title: Default and named parameters

44.title: Lambda Functions

45.title: As a type

46.body: IO is implemented with some built-in functions.
46.title: IO

47.body: Print can be used to send text into standard out or standard error. Input is used to get input from the user fXrom standard in.
47.title: Standard Streams

48.body: WriteFile can be used to write text in to a file. ReadFile can be used to read text from a file. WriteBytes can be used to write bytes in to a file. ReadBytes can be used to read bytes from a file.
48.title: File IO

49.body: Exec can be used to execute a command.
49.title: Process IO

5.body:
the interp binary can also spin up an interactive session (a Read-Eval-Print-Loop). $ interp
> Print("Hello, world!")
Hello, world! To execute a file simply use imp. > imp "hello.it"
Hello, world!
5.title: Repl

50.body:
Print is a versatile function. Print a value to standard out followed by a newline. Print(1); Print also can take a second argument, an object that contains options. Print( 1, newline=false, # Optional, defaults to true stream="stderr" # Optional, "stdout" or "stderr", defaults to "stdout",
);
50.title: Print Function

51.title: User Input

52.body:
Interp defines two functions for reading and writing bytes from and to a file. WriteBytes("text.txt", [55, 54]);
var bytes = ReadBytes("test.txt");
52.title: Byte IO

53.body:
With the Exec function you can execute an executable with arguments, and get the standard output as a String . Print(Exec("echo", "Hello, world!");)
# output: Hello, world!
53.title: Exec

54.title: If Statement

55.title: The While Loop

56.title: For Loop

57.body: The following simple types are pre-defined: Any type matches any value except Void . Void type matches Void . Null type matches Null . String type matches Strings . Int type matches Int . Float type matches Floats . Bool type matches Booleans . List type, read more here . Function type, read more here
57.title: Types

58.body: And and Or Types are also allowed 1 is Int or String # true
58.title: And and Or Types

59.title: Structs

6.body: A good place to start is to look at the various examples ! Happy coding :)
6.title: Where next?

60.title: Enums

61.title: Atoms

62.body:
Modules group code into namespaces. mod math { fun Sin(n Float) Float { ... } fun Cos(n Float) Float { ... } } Module members can be accessed anywhere with the full path specified. Print(math::Sin(2.3)); Use statements (unique to each file) can be used to bring module members into the global namespace. use math;
Print(Sin(2.3));
62.title: Modules

63.body: Interp supports real multi-threading with the ability to send messages to and from threads.
63.title: Thread

64.title: Simple Use

65.body:
Each thread in Interp has it's own virtual machine. There is no GIL like in Python or Ruby. This means that memory is NOT shared! You will have to import all libraries separately. imp "lib.it"; var thread = do Thread() { # this is needed here to use functions from 'lib.it' within the thread imp "lib.it";
}
65.title: Separate Virtual Machines

66.body:
var thread = do x, y in Thread(1, 2) { if x == 1 & y == 2 { Print("Yes, they are equal!"); }
} Join(thread);
66.title: Thread Arguments

67.title: Messags

68.body:
The imp statement can be used to include Interp code. imp_this.it: Print("Hello"); main.it: imp "imp_this.it"
# output: Hello You can also import a String variable. Note: Imports are cached, you can't import the same file twice.
68.title: Import

69.body: Comments are single-line and start with # Print("Hello!") # comment Read more about Strings or Print
69.title: Comments

71.body: Pure Rust functions can be called from Interp.
71.title: Foreign Functions

72.body:
This function can be used to import a foreign function. var MyForeignFunc = ImportForeign( "path_to_shared_object", "ForeignFunc"
); MyForeignFunc();
72.title: ImportForeign
